caveats: a: OEWS is available at NAICS 611600 rather than 611620 and excludes self-employed instructors.
a: The professional-coaching survey is not limited to athletic coaches or the United States.
a: Exposure includes partial task assistance and avoidable hiring, not wholesale coach replacement.
rho: Published evidence demonstrates use and capability rather than sustained labor removal.
rho: Child privacy, biometric video data, injury risk, and safeguarding require human review and controlled deployment.
rho: Implementation will vary sharply between standardized technique sports and open-play team sports.
e: The supplied LMM firm count is an estimate based on size classes and a margin bridge.
e: NAICS boundaries distinguish instruction from facilities and leagues, but real firms often mix those activities.
e: A swim-school platform demonstrates eligibility for one facility-intensive segment and may not generalize to all sports.
s5: Gallup covers all industries and owner-reported intentions rather than completed deals.
s5: The disclosed swim-school acquisition does not provide a denominator or terms.
s5: Founder reputation, credentials, and customer relationships can prevent an otherwise planned transfer.
q: No public source directly measures automation pass-through or renewal repricing in sports instruction.
q: Private lessons, group classes, memberships, camps, and institutional contracts have different retention dynamics.
q: Facility-constrained schools may reinvest savings in capacity or safety rather than report them as margin.
d5: Participation, occupation employment, and broad-industry employment are not direct measures of 611620 service quantity.
d5: The 1.2% participation increase is a single-year national observation and follows faster post-2020 growth.
d5: Teen inactivity rose in 2025 even as total participation increased.
o: No source directly measures operator displacement across 611620.
o: Operator need is higher for children and safety-critical sports than for healthy adults doing self-directed fitness.
o: Rapid advances in multimodal motion analysis and connected equipment would push the estimate downward.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily household-paid recurring class tuition, memberships, lesson packages, seasonal programs, and repeat camps, with some school, club, or municipal contracts; facilities and equipment may be owned or leased and instructor labor is central to delivery.
lens.excluded_activities: Fitness centers whose primary service is facility access, sports leagues and spectator teams, recreational camps where instruction is incidental, academic schools, freelance instructors without transferable operations, captive programs, and sports equipment retailers.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market schools, academies, and instruction camps that repeatedly provide paid athletic instruction to external customers, including swimming, gymnastics, martial arts, cheerleading, riding, golf, tennis, and team-sport skills.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment and wages for the broader four-digit Other Schools and Instruction industry rather than 611620, while a professional-coaching survey covers coaching beyond sports. I use the BLS mix to approximate wage weights and apply task exposure suggested by coaching uses of GenAI: high for administration, communications, content, and research; low for live physical demonstration, supervision, safety, and relationship work. The broader industry includes tutoring and less physical instruction, likely biasing the mechanical exposure estimate upward for sports instruction.
caveats: OEWS is available at NAICS 611600 rather than 611620 and excludes self-employed instructors.
The professional-coaching survey is not limited to athletic coaches or the United States.
Exposure includes partial task assistance and avoidable hiring, not wholesale coach replacement.
a.change_evidence: A 611620-specific occupation payroll panel combined with time diaries from swim, gymnastics, martial-arts, riding, racquet-sport, and team-sport academies, coded task by task for current automation, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: In broader NAICS 611600, educational occupations are 51.97% of employment, sports and media occupations 13.30%, office support 9.87%, management 6.89%, and business operations 3.14%. Coaches can automate practice-plan drafts, video tagging, progress summaries, marketing, enrollment, scheduling, billing, and routine parent messages, but live observation, correction, motivation, safeguarding, and emergency response remain human tasks. The resulting wage-weighted exposed share is a little over one-fifth.

d5.bridge: SFIA measures participation in 126 US sports, fitness, and leisure activities, not purchases of instruction, while BLS projects national employment for coaches, fitness instructors, and the broader private Other Schools and Instruction industry. I use recent 1.2% participation growth and six-to-twelve-percent ten-year occupation growth as positive anchors, temper them for affordability, youth demographics, capacity, and the fact that participation need not involve paid instruction. Participation growth may overstate instructional demand if activity is self-directed, while coach employment may understate demand if productivity rises.
caveats: Participation, occupation employment, and broad-industry employment are not direct measures of 611620 service quantity.
The 1.2% participation increase is a single-year national observation and follows faster post-2020 growth.
Teen inactivity rose in 2025 even as total participation increased.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of paid enrollments, instructional hours, utilization, retention, and real prices from swim, martial-arts, gymnastics, riding, and youth-sports management vendors, segmented by age and sport, would materially change this range.
d5.rationale: US sport and fitness participation reached 250 million people in 2025 and grew 1.2% year over year, while BLS projects coaches and scouts to grow 6.4% and exercise trainers 11.9% from 2024 to 2034. Private Other Schools and Instruction employment is projected to rise 7.0% over the decade. Paid instruction should therefore grow modestly in real, quality-adjusted quantity, though teen inactivity, household affordability, and regional youth-population pressure cap the base.

caveats: The supplied LMM firm count is an estimate based on size classes and a margin bridge.
NAICS boundaries distinguish instruction from facilities and leagues, but real firms often mix those activities.
A swim-school platform demonstrates eligibility for one facility-intensive segment and may not generalize to all sports.
e.change_evidence: A stratified Data Axle or PitchBook census of 611620 LMM firms matched to websites, customer billing cadence, service mix, instructor count, owner teaching hours, facility economics, and tax status would reveal the actual eligible share.
e.rationale: Most LMM-scale businesses correctly classified in 611620 provide repeat external instruction and therefore fit the default lens. Exclusions arise from one-off camps without repeat relationships, founder-only coaching practices, incidental instruction attached to facilities or leagues, grant-dependent programs, and businesses whose transferable economics principally reside in real estate or equipment rather than instruction.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 611620 ($4,334,929,367) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 611620 ($11,707,022,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1657 (margins.json[61]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Education EBITDA/Sales 16.57%); revenue/employee $73,453 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 611620); class <5: 11,106 firms, avg revenue/firm $240,504 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $39,852 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,238 firms, avg revenue/firm $510,368 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $84,568 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,355 firms, avg revenue/firm $760,107 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $125,950 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,888 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,755,034 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $290,809 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 102 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,236,049 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,530,413 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $70,409,211 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $11,666,806 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 102

caveats: No source directly measures operator displacement across 611620.
Operator need is higher for children and safety-critical sports than for healthy adults doing self-directed fitness.
Rapid advances in multimodal motion analysis and connected equipment would push the estimate downward.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal customer switching and outcome data comparing human-led, hybrid, and AI-only sports instruction—especially renewal, injury, skill progression, and willingness to pay by sport and age—would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five paid quantity should still require an accountable operator because customers purchase live observation, physical correction, motivation, group management, access to specialized facilities, credentialing, safeguarding, and safety response. AI plans, apps, wearables, and computer vision can fully self-serve some adult fitness, running, golf, or technique practice, but current general-purpose workout advice can be incomplete and does not reliably interpret injury or human context. Human-led hybrid delivery remains the likely dominant model.

caveats: No public source directly measures automation pass-through or renewal repricing in sports instruction.
Private lessons, group classes, memberships, camps, and institutional contracts have different retention dynamics.
Facility-constrained schools may reinvest savings in capacity or safety rather than report them as margin.
q.change_evidence: School-level panels of cohort prices, discounts, class ratios, coach compensation, churn, and contribution margins before and after documented workflow automation, supplemented by local competitor price scraping, would materially narrow retention.
q.rationale: Household tuition, memberships, lesson packages, and camp fees are generally fixed customer prices rather than cost-plus contracts, so avoided administrative hiring or improved coach utilization can initially remain with the operator. Over five years, visible scheduling efficiency, local price competition, franchise or facility fees, instructor wage pressure, and customer expectations for lower ratios or better programming share part of the benefit.

rho.bridge: The coaching study measures current use and attitudes among 205 professional coaches, not five-year implementation in US sports-instruction firms, while the figure-skating example demonstrates a technical capability in one elite sport. I translate adoption for research, content, and administration plus demonstrated mobile computer vision into implementation of the exposed opportunity, then discount for fragmented systems, minor privacy, liability, coach training, and sport-specific data needs. Elite-sport technology may overstate near-term small-school capability, but five years allows diffusion into packaged products.
caveats: Published evidence demonstrates use and capability rather than sustained labor removal.
Child privacy, biometric video data, injury risk, and safeguarding require human review and controlled deployment.
Implementation will vary sharply between standardized technique sports and open-play team sports.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry and audited before-and-after labor data from sports-school management and video-analysis platforms, segmented by sport and firm size and including false-feedback, incident, and parent-complaint rates, would materially move implementation.
rho.rationale: GenAI is already used in coaching workflows for research, content creation, and administrative support, while relational and interpretive work remains limited. Mobile computer vision can provide immediate technical metrics in figure skating, showing that some sport-specific analysis is implementable without specialized sensors. Integrated enrollment, communications, payments, practice planning, and video review should capture a majority—but not all—of the exposed opportunity within five years.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year plans across all US employer-business owners, with 22% planning a sale or transfer, not completed qualifying control transfers in 611620. I haircut that intention rate for noncompletion, family gifts and internal reorganizations that may not qualify, while allowing for demonstrated strategic acquisitions of multi-location swim schools. Gallup likely overstates completed qualifying transfers; one disclosed platform acquisition may make the active segments look more liquid than the full industry.
caveats: Gallup covers all industries and owner-reported intentions rather than completed deals.
The disclosed swim-school acquisition does not provide a denominator or terms.
Founder reputation, credentials, and customer relationships can prevent an otherwise planned transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Closed 611620 transaction counts from PitchBook, DealStats, BizBuySell, Axial, SBA change-of-ownership loans, and state ownership records, divided by an eligible-firm panel and paired with owner-age interviews, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Broad employer-owner succession evidence supplies a five-year upper anchor, and a 2024 acquisition of an eight-location Florida swim-school chain by a 60-plus-location operator confirms strategic demand for standardized, multi-site instruction. However, many academies remain coach- or founder-dependent, and intentions frequently fail to close, supporting a completed-transfer probability in the mid-teens.

principal_driver: Recurring instruction demand and positive participation trends allow integrated AI administration and analysis to raise coach utilization without removing the live service.
principal_weakness: Safety-critical embodied delivery limits labor substitution, while founder dependence and facility obligations can impair transferability.
